title: A Deadly Tapeworm Just Hit the Pacific Northwest — Found in 37% of Coyotes
script: Deadly tapeworm hits Pacific Northwest! Scientists at the University of Washington just confirmed a major finding: Echinococcus multilocularis — the fox tapeworm — has been found in 37 percent of coyotes tested around Puget Sound. This is the first time it's ever been detected in wild animals on the US West Coast. The parasite has spent decades spreading from Europe through Canada and the American Midwest. Now it's in Washington. In coyotes and foxes, it causes no symptoms. But if it reaches dogs or humans — through contaminated soil or animal feces — it can cause alveolar echinococcosis, a disease that grows slow cancer-like cysts through the body. Symptoms can hide for 5 to 15 years. No human cases have been reported on the West Coast yet. Could your dog be the first to bring it home?
